State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662 322-5660 322-5901 322-6441 322-6444

August 6, 1982

Ms. Evelyn M. Finn
Pasadena City Attorney
100 North Garfield Avenue
Pasadena, CA 91109

Re: Our No. A-82-117

Dear Ms. Finn:

Thank you for your letter requesting advice on behalf
of the City of Pasadena. The facts, as I understand them,
are that the City is considering amending its Zoning Plan
and Code to allow the creation of Landmark Districts in
areas of the City which are architecturally and/or
historically significant. Each Landmark District must be
composed of at least contiguous1/ properties which meet
the requirements for listing in the National Register, or
which are essential to the integrity of the area.

Each Landmark District will have a Landmark Design
Review Committee composed of one member each from the
Cultural Heritage Commission, the Planning Commission and
the Design Review Committee, as well as an architect, a
realtor and an owner of property within the District (to be
referred to as an "owner-representative").

The function of a Review Committee is to maintain
harmony among the structures in an area, paying particular
attention to their exterior design and such things as
scale, height, spacing, roof shape, style, materials,
colors and texture. To fulfill this goal, the Review
Committee must approve any demolition, removal,

_______________________

1/ Contiguous properties include those separated by
streets, alleys and other public right of ways.

Ms. Evelyn M. Finn
August 6, 1982
Page 2


construction, alteration, move or change in the appearance
of any structure, portion of a structure, or other natural
or man-made feature visible from the street.

    In order to avoid any conflicts of interest by the
owner-representatives sitting on the Review Committees, the
City proposes that these positions be non-voting. Your
question is whether this is the correct approach under the
Political Reform Act.

                          Discussion

    The Political Reform Act does not require that the
positions held by the owner-representatives be non-voting.
Rather, the Act requires individual public officials to
disqualify themselves from specific decisions in which they
have a conflict of interest. The determination as to
whether or not disqualification is required can only be
made after considering the facts surrounding each decision.

    The conflict of interest provisions of the Act prohibit
a public official from making, or participating in the
making of, any governmental decision in which the official
knows or has reason to know that he or she has a financial
interest. (Government Code Section 871002/.) This
prohibition applies to all of the members of the Design
Review Committees, not just the owner-representatives (See
Section 82048) and it prohibits them not only from voting
on a decision, but also from participating, in any way, in
the decision making process.

    The individual Review Committee members have a
"financial interest" in a decision if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material
effect,3/ distinguishable from its effect on the public


_____________________

    2/ Hereinafter all statutory references are to the
Government Code unless otherwise indicated.

    3/ See the enclosed copy of 2 California
Administrative Code Section 18702 for the meaning of
"material financial effect."

Ms. Evelyn M. Finn
August 6, 1982
Page 3


generally, on the types of financial interests described in
Section 87103(a)-(d).4/ The effect of a decision on a
Committee member's interest is distinguishable from its
effect on the public generally unless the decision will
affect the official's interest in substantially the same
manner as it will affect all members of the public or a
significant segment of the public. (2 California
Administrative Code Section 18703.)

In this present situation, the "public generally" is
comprised of those persons within the jurisdiction of each
Landmark Review Committee, i.e., the residents of each
Landmark District. As a general rule, landowners within a
jurisdiction constitute a significant segment of the public
generally, thus, if the owner-representative is confronted
with a decision which will materially affect his or her
financial interests in substantially the same manner as it
will affect all of the other landowners in the District,
then the official is not required to disqualify himself or
herself.5/

For example, a decision which has the effect of
conferring a financial benefit or detriment on a Committee
member of $100 or more is said to be a decision having a

_______________________

4/ Section 87103:

(a) Any business entity in which the member has a
direct or indirect investment worth more than $1,000;

(b) Any real property interest in which the member has
a direct or indirect interest worth more than $1,000;

(c) Any source of income aggregating $250 or more,
received by or promised to the member within 12 months
prior to when the decision is made; or

(d) Any business entity in which the member is a
director, officer, partner, trustee, employee or holds any
position of management.

5/ The issue of what is a significant segment of the
public generally has been addressed by the Commission in
the Owens Opinion and the Georgene Barnes letter, copies
enclosed.

Ms. Evelyn M. Finn
August 6, 1982
Page 4


material financial effect on the official, normally
requiring disqualification. (Section 87103(c) and 2
California Administrative Code Section 18702(b)(3)(A).)
However, if the decision in question is whether or not to
require all of the landowners in a District to construct a
matching awning on the front of their building at a cost of
$100 each, then all of the landowners are similarly
affected and the owner-representative is not required to
disqualify himself or herself from making, or participating
in the decision.

     If a decision will have an effect on a Committee
member's interest that is distinguishable from its effect
on the public generally or a significant segment of the
public, and if it is reasonably foreseeable that the effect
on the official's interest will be a material one, then the
official is required to disqualify himself or herself from
making, or participating in the making of, the decision.6/

     For example, if the Review Committee is considering
whether or not to allow an owner-representative to build on
his or her own property, disqualification is required
because such a decision will foreseeably affect the fair
market value of the property to the extent described in 2
California Administrative Code Section 18702(b)(2).7/

     Many of the decisions that the Review Committees will
consider are not the type of decisions which will
materially affect the members' financial interests.  For
instance, a decision on whether to forbid an owner-
representative from painting his or her home green, and to
require the owner, instead, to paint it white, is a
decision having a negligible effect on the owner's
financial interests.  Thus, disqualification is not
prescribed.

     Finally, if the City of Pasadena chose to keep the
owner-representative positions non-voting, this will not be
sufficient to avoid conflicts of interests under the Act.
If disqualification is required, an owner-representative
may not make, or participate in the making of, a decision.
2 California Administrative Code Section 18700(c) defines
the type of conduct which is included in this prohibition.

_________________

     6/  For the specific amounts, see 2 California
Administrative Code Section 18702(b)(2).

     7/  See footnote 3.

Ms. Evelyn M. Finn
August 6, 1982
Page 5


     I hope that I have clarified the requirements of the
Political Reform Act for you. In my hypotheticals, I have
applied the disqualification provisions of the Act only in
the context of the owner-representatives, but please
remember that the other members of the Review Committees
are also subject to these provisions. I am including a
letter I wrote to the Pasadena City Attorney, Victor
Kaleta, in November 1981, which I feel will be helpful to
you in applying the disqualification provisions to the
other Committee members.

     If you have any additional questions, please feel free
to contact me at 916/322-5901.

                                        Very truly yours,

                                        [signature]
                                        Janis Shank McLean
                                        Counsel
                                        Legal Division

Enclosures

City of Pasadena
ONE HUNDRED NORTH GARFIELD AVENUE
PASADENA, CALIFORNIA 91109

OFFICE OF CITY ATTORNEY
ROOM 202, CITY HALL
PHONE: (213) 577-4141

June 21, 1982

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA  95804

Attention Legal Division

The City of Pasadena is in the process of hearing an amendment to its Zoning Plan and Code to add a chapter dealing with how a Landmark District may be created, its criteria and how matters relating thereto will be heard. This is a kind of floating or overlay zone which will apply to properties therein in addition to its basic land use zoning. A copy of the proposed text is enclosed for reference, along with an outline of the ordinance's evolution and other pertinent documents.

Previous drafts have included property owners within the "District" on the review board and there was no minimum number of properties. We have objected on the basis that an obvious and inevitable conflict would exist except in a large area and where the property being reviewed is sufficiently remote from the committee member's property so as to avoid an effect on value. The ordinance therefore was amended to require a minimum of 10 lots and the property owner would be non-voting (see section 090). We are prepared to cope with the remaining "influence questions" remaining in such a non-voting setup. However, proponents of these districts are fighting to have restored voting rights to a representative-owner or representatives-owners of the District. They believe it is commonly part of such Cultural Heritage or Landmark Districts ordinances in other cities including those in California.

Will you please give us the benefit of an opinion as to whether you agree with this approach we have taken. Also we would appreciate knowing whether you have rendered any opinion or

Page 2 - Fair Political Practices
Commission

June 21, 1982

ruling on other city Landmark Districts with similarly interested members.

Thank you for your assistance.

Very truly yours

Evelynn M. Finn
Evelynn M. Finn
City Attorney

Evelynn M. Finn
City Attorney
City of Pasadena
One Hundred North Garfield Ave.
Pasadena, CA  91109

                                                          Re: A-82-117

Dear Ms. Finn:

     Your letter requesting advice under the Political
Reform Act has been referred to Janis Shank McLean, an
attorney in the Legal Division of the Fair Political
Practices Commission.  If you have any questions about your
advice request, you may contact this attorney directly at
(916) 322-5901.

     We try to answer all advice requests promptly.
Therefore, unless your request poses particularly complex
legal questions, or unless more information is needed to
answer your request, you should expect a response within 21
working days.

                                          Very truly yours,


                                          [illegible] Milman
                                          Barbara A. Milman
                                          Chief
                                          Legal Division

Agenda Report

TO: Board of Permits
FROM: City Manager
SUBJECT: Landmark District Overlay Zone Ordinance

Date: June 3, 1982

Summary:

This is supplementary information to the Agenda Report dated 5/5/82.

Background:

In response to Board questions and public comments, staff has prepared
the following:

1 - A chronology explaining the evolution of the ordinance
    over the past seven months.

2 - A flow chart explaining how a District might be adopted.

3 - A map indicating areas that might be eligible for Landmark
    District designation.

Respectfully submitted,

[signature] JMD

DONALD F. McINTYRE, City Manager

Prepared by:

[signature]
TERRY E. TORNEK
Director of Planning

Approved:

[signature]
DANIEL H. VASSAR
Director, HDA

Team
Attachments

[illegible]

[illegible]

Assessment 1
Ordinance Evolution

LANDMARK DISTRICT

DRAFT #1 November 11, 1981

1. This proposed ordinance was intended to follow the concept established in
the 1979 Landmark District Ordinance (attached) which was supported by the Board,
but due to legal problems associated with the processing of the permit applications
it was never implemented.

- Title of proposed ordinance "Neighborhood Conservation Zone".
- District established by petition bearing signatures of not less than 30%
of the owners of property or 50% of the households within district.
- Time to 60 days.
- Review Committee made up of two district residents and three members of the
City's Cultural Heritage Commission.

DRAFT # 2  December 9, 1981

Following the November report, the City Attorney reviewed the proposal and made
the following changes:

- The phrase "30% of the owners of property" to "the owners
of 30% of the property".
- A minimum size for VC Zone is established at 10 lots.
- A five year Capital Improvement Program for the District,
which was a required element of the Conservation Plan, was
deleted.
- A new section was added which required that the Conservation
Plan be approved by the Board.
- District representation on the Design Review Committee was
determined to have a potential for conflict of interest and
so was removed.
- Under the Duty to Maintain Structures and Premises, Section
190, the following sentence was deleted: "Where a dispute
exists as to whether a particular action, alteration, etc.,
constitutes maintenance, such dispute shall be resolved
by the Design Review Committee.
- The precedence of an approved Conservation Plan over the
general provisions of the chapter was ruled illegal and
was deleted (Sec.200).
- The Design Review Committee was changed to "Review Committee"
to keep it separate from other design review functions.

DRAFT #2.5    March 5, 1982

At the December 9, 1981 public hearing, the Planning Commission reviewed the second
draft and made the following changes:

- Name changed to "Landmark District"
- Establishment of district changed to require " the signatures
of the owners of not less than 50% of the households in the proposed district..."
- A finding required to establish a District, was deleted.    "There
is substantial support within the proposed district for
adoption of such protective devices".
- The review Committee make-up was changed to two members from
the Cultural Heritage Commission and one member from the
Planning Commission.

DRAFT #3  March 8, 1982

On March 5, the Planning Commission held a workshop meeting to discuss the Draft
#2.5. Both developers and members of the Cultural Heritage Commission participated.
The comments made at this meeting were incorporated into a 3rd draft. The major
changes are as follows:

- Inclusion of required compliance to Housing and Land Use
Elements.
- Significant findings limited to architectural or historical
basis.

-1-

Ordinance Evolution

Landmark District (Continued)

DRAFT #3 March 3, 1982 (continued)

- Review Committee take-up changed to include the member
  each from the Cultural Heritage Commission, Planning
  Commission, Design Review Committee, an architect, realtor
  and non-voting property owner.
- Inclusion of section which clarifies status of projects
  commenced prior to creation of it.
- Inclusion of section identifying enforcement and penalty
  provisions.
- Fee changed to equal the base fee for a zone change
  application.

DRAFT #4 April 21, 1982

On April 14, the Planning Commission held a public hearing on the 3rd Draft. Based
on comments made, staff prepared Draft #4. Following is a list of changes made:
- Inclusion of eligibility criteria
- Clarification of what constitutes consensus.
- Addition of the following sentence to the Conservation Plan
  Section: "following staff review of the Conservation Plan
  if it is determined the provisions contained in the plan
  constitute a major change in the underlying zone, the
  Landmark District zone change shall include the change to
  the appropriate underlying zone and full zone change fee
  shall be collected.
- Economic hardship waiver section added.
- Revocation of District section added.

DRAFT #5 April 29, 1982

The only change made in this draft is under Section 38C, from "... a zone change
application shall be entertained" to "... a zone change application shall be required".

Agenda Report

TO          BOARD OF DIRECTORS                Date    May 5, 1982
FROM        City Manager
SUBJECT     Landmark District Overlay Zone Ordinance


RECOMMENDATION:

It is recommended that the Board:

1. Hold a public hearing on the proposed amendment to the Zoning
   Ordinance regarding procedures for the creation of a Landmark
   District Overlay Zone;

2. Following the hearing, certify the Initial Environmental
   Study and Negative Declaration prepared for the proposed
   amendment; and

3. Adopt the amendment to the Zoning Ordinance related to the
   Landmark District Overlay Zone.


SUMMARY:

The intent of the proposed ordinance is to create an overlay zone designed
to preserve and promote the character of established neighborhoods. Major
features of the ordinance include the following:

1. A proposal for a Landmark District is initiated only by a
   petition of 50% of the owners of 10 or more contiguous lots.

2. For each Landmark District a Conservation Plan is submitted with
   the petition. The plan is to include a description of the pro-
   posed district, a statement of the goals and objectives of the
   district, a list of specific alterations subject to design
   review, performance guidelines for new construction and altera-
   tions, and rules and regulations for design review.

3. The Cultural Heritage Commission determines and recommends to the
   Planning Commission whether the proposed properties and district
   meet the eligibility criteria for listing on the National Regis-
   ter or if the properties are essential to the integrity of the
   district.

4. The Planning Commission reviews the proposed Landmark District
   and Conservation Plan and make a recommendation to the Board.

5. Once a Landmark District and Conservation Plan are approved, the
   Board appoints a Landmark District Design Review Committee which
   will be responsible for making determinations on requests for
   certificates of Appropriateness as it relates to the Conserva-
   tion Plan guidelines.

[illegible] May 5, 1982

[illegible]

TO:         Board of Directors                                    May 5, 1982

FROM:       Planning Commission

SUBJECT:    Landmark District Overlay Zone Ordinance


RECOMMENDATION:

It is recommended that following the public hearing, the Board adopt the pro-
posed amendment to the Zoning Ordinance which will establish the Landmark
District Overlay Zone.


BACKGROUND:

At the December 9, 1981 public hearing, there were four people who spoke in
favor and three who spoke in opposition to the proposed ordinance. The Plan-
ning Commission questioned whether tenant participation in the creation of
a district was appropriate. They also felt that the $1.00 filing fee was
too low. Finally, the Commission directed staff to clarify the criteria
used to define the term "significant".

The Planning Commission held a workshop meeting on March 6, 1982 to review
the second draft which incorporated many of the concerns expressed at the
December public hearing. Interested citizens also participated in the
meeting which involved a line by line analysis.

The third draft was reviewed at the April 14, 1982 public hearing. The
changes in this draft included:

     1.  Inclusion of required compliance to Housing and Land Use Elements
         of the General Plan.

     2.  Required findings to create a district to include architectural
         or historical significance.

     3.  A change in the makeup of the Design Review Committee membership
         to include a representative from the Cultural Heritage Commission,
         Planning Commission, and Design Review Committee, an architect,
         realtor and non-voting property owner.

     4.  Inclusion of a section which clarifies status of projects com-
         menced prior to creation of a district.

     5.  Inclusion of a section on enforcement and penalty provisions.

After receiving public testimony, the Planning Commission closed the hearing
and deferred the decision to the April 28 special business meeting. Staff
was directed to clarify the eligibility criteria, definition of the term
"contiguous" and to include a revocation procedure.

On April 28, 1982 the Planning Commission approved the proposed amendment
with the additional change that a zone change application shall be required
if major changes to the underlying zone are proposed.

Following further discussion a motion was made to recommend to the Board
approval. All commissioners voted in favor except for Commissioner Judd
who felt there would be too much potential for abuse.

                                                    Respectfully submitted,


                                                    [signature]
                                                    VICTOR LEE, Chairman
                                                    City Planning Commission

VAE:ml
LMDS6.42

Draft No. : 4-29-92
Planning Department

LANDMARK DISTRICT

010 General

Except as in this title specifically provided and allowed, the following

regulations shall apply to Landmark District zones.

020 Purpose

The Landmark District Zone is intended to be an overlay zone which

will:

A. Implement the City's General Plan by assuring appropriate development

consistent with the Urban Design, Neighborhood Enhancement, Housing, Land

Use and Historic and Cultural Resources Elements thereof;

B. Deter the demolition, destruction, alteration, misuse and neglect of

architecturally and significant buildings which represent an important link

to Pasadena's past;

C. Promote the conservation, preservation, protection and enhancement of

the Landmark District;

D. Stimulate the economic wealth and residential quality of the community

and stabilize and enhance the value of property;

E. Be tailored to the character and significance to each area of applica-

tion through a Conservation Plan which includes goals, objectives and

design criteria.

025 Eligibility

Properties and Districts meeting the criteria for listing on the National

Register shall be eligible for inclusion in a Landmark District. Other properties

may be included in a District if they are essential to the integrity of the District.

030 Establishment of Landmark Districts

Reclassification of property in Zone 12 may be initiated by a petition
requesting establishment of the District, bearing the signature of owners of
not less than fifty (50) percent of the parcels in the proposed District may be
filed with the Planning Commission. Each parcel developed with condominiums
shall count as one signature only. The petition must contain the proposed Con-
servation Plan for the District as described in Section 380. The Commission may
require such other information or maps as it may deem necessary. No District
shall be established which contains fewer than ten contiguous eligible legal land
parcels. Contiguity shall include parcels separated by streets, alleys and other
public rights-of-way.

040 Filing Petitions

A petition for a Landmark District may be filed with the Planning Commission
at any time and must be heard by the Planning Commission within 90 days from the
date of filing. A copy of such application shall be sent to the Cultural Heritage
Commission for its determination and findings of architectural or historical signifi-
cance using the eligibility criteria applied to National Register nominations. The
filing fee for a Landmark District Ordinance shall be the base fee for that charged
for a zone change application.

050 Public Hearing - Required

Application for Landmark District shall be heard by the Planning Commission
and the Board of Directors in accordance with the provisions of Chapter
17.66.

060 Public Hearing - Notice

Notice of public hearings for establishment of a Landmark District shall
be given in the manner prescribed in Sections 17.62.090 (A) and 17.66.050,
of the Zoning Ordinance and by registered mail to persons to whom is assessed
each property within the proposed district.

-2-

070 Findings Required in Landmark District

Before adopting any Landmark District, the Planning Commission and the
City Board of Directors shall make each of the following findings:

1. The proposed District and Conservation Plan are consistent with the
goals and objectives of the General Plan, including the Urban Design, the
Historic and Cultural Resources, Neighborhood Enhancement, Land Use and
Housing Elements;

2. The proposed District is consistent with the purposes of this Chapter;

3. The proposed District has a significant architectural or historic
character which can be preserved and enhanced through appropriate
controls on new development and alterations to existing buildings.

080 Conservation Plan

The Conservation Plan shall be prepared by the applicant with the assistance
of the Planning Department. For each Landmark District, a Conservation Plan
shall be prepared which shall contain:

1. A description of the area involved including the boundaries,
setting, age, and character of the structures, urban design elements,
streetscapes and major public improvements;

2. A statement of the architectural or historic significance of the
area;

3. A statement of the Goals and Objectives of the District;

4. A list of specific alterations subject to design review (e.g.,
exterior scale, materials, color, texture, rhythm or proportion of
structures and landscape elements);

5. Performance Guidelines for new construction and alterations within
the District;

6. Rules and regulations for design review;

Amendments to the Conservation Plan, except minor procedural amendments shall
be processed in accordance with the provisions of Sections 040 through 060 of
this chapter.

-1-

In Jones LD, the regulations governing the uses of land and structures,
the weight of buildings, and requirements for lot area, width, and yards
shall be as set forth in the underlying zoning district s) except as may
be expressly modified by the Conservation Plan for that District. If there are
to be major ranges to the underlying zone, a zone change application shall be
required. Following staff review of the Conservation Plan, if it is deter-
mined the provisions contained in the Plan constitute a major change in the
underlying zone the Landmark District zone change shall include the change to
the appropriate underlying zone and full zone change fees shall be collected.

085 Approval of Conservation Plan
No Conservation Plan shall be in force or effect until an ordinance
establishing the Landmark District is adopted by the City Board of Directors.

090 Landmark District Design Review Committee
A Landmark District Design Review Committee shall be established by the Board
of Directors consisting of one member each from the Cultural Heritage Commission,
the Planning Commission and the Design Review Committee as well as an architect,
a realtor, and a non-voting property owner from each district.

100 Procedural Rules and Regulations
In addition to any other functions set forth in this title, the Landmark
Design Review Committee shall formulate procedural rules and regulations deemed
necessary or convenient to carry out the provisions of the Conservation Plan.
Such rules and regulations shall take effect upon approval by the Board of
Directors and filing with the City Clerk.

110 Demolition, Removal, Alteration, or Construction Prohibited
After adoption of a Landmark District, no person shall demolish, remove,
construct, alter, move, or change the appearance of any structure, portion of a
structure or other natural or man-made feature visible from a public street or
way without first having

-4-

applied for and open issued a Certificate of Appropriateness by the Landmark
District Design Review Committee.. This section shall not be applicable to
interior alterations to structures or changes which do not materially affect the
structure's or site's appearance from a public street or way. Nothing in this
section shall be construed to affect ordinary maintenance and repair.

120 Application for Certificate of Appropriateness

An Application for a Certificate of Appropriateness shall be filed with
the Director of Housing and Community Development. The application shall be
in the form and shall contain such information and materials as are prescribed
in any duly adopted rules and regulations. The said Director may require that
the application be supplemented by such additional information or materials as
in his opinion may be necessary for a complete review by the Landmark District
Design Review Committee. The Landmark District Design Review Committee shall
have 30 days from the date on which the said Director accepts the application
as complete within which to review, approve, conditionally approve, or deny the
application. No Certificate of Appropriateness shall be issued or be of any
force or effect without the prior review and approval of the Landmark District
Design Review Committee. By mutual consent, given in writing by the applicant,
the Landmark District Design Review Committee may extend the review period.

130 Landmark District Design Review Process

A. The Landmark District Design Review Committee shall, in accordance
with the provisions of this Chapter and any duly adopted rules and regulations, review all applications for Certificates of Appropriateness.

B. To assure harmonious relationships between existing structures, and
to achieve the purpose of the Zone, in reviewing and rendering its
decision upon an application, the Landmark District Design Review Committee
shall consider

-5-

sections.conclusion: This is supplementary information to the Agenda Report dated 5/5/82.
sections.facts:
In response to Board questions and public comments, staff has prepared the following:

1 - A chronology explaining the evolution of the ordinance over the past seven months.
2 - A flow chart explaining how a District might be adopted.
3 - A map indicating areas that might be eligible for Landmark District designation.
sections.analysis:
The Political Reform Act does not require that the positions held by the owner-representatives be non-voting. Rather, the Act requires individual public officials to disqualify themselves from specific decisions in which they have a conflict of interest. The determination as to whether or not disqualification is required can only be made after considering the facts surrounding each decision.

The conflict of interest provisions of the Act prohibit a public official from making, or participating in the making of, any governmental decision in which the official knows or has reason to know that he or she has a financial interest. (Government Code Section 871002/.) This prohibition applies to all of the members of the Design Review Committees, not just the owner-representatives (See Section 82048) and it prohibits them not only from voting on a decision, but also from participating, in any way, in the decision making process.

The individual Review Committee members have a "financial interest" in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material effect,3/ distinguishable from its effect on the public

2/ Hereinafter 3/ See the enclosed copy of 2 California Administrative Code Section 18702 for the meaning of "material financial effect."

Ms. Evelyn M. Finn  
August 6, 1982  

generally, on the types of financial interests described in Section 87103(a)-(d).4/ The effect of a decision on a Committee member's interest is distinguishable from its effect on the public generally unless the decision will affect the official's interest in substantially the same manner as it will affect all members of the public or a significant segment of the public. (2 California Administrative Code Section 18703.)

In this present situation, the "public generally" is comprised of those persons within the jurisdiction of each Landmark Review Committee, i.e., the residents of each Landmark District. As a general rule, landowners within a jurisdiction constitute a significant segment of the public generally, thus, if the owner-representative is confronted with a decision which will materially affect his or her financial interests in substantially the same manner as it will effect all of the other landowners in the District, then the official is not required to disqualify himself or herself.5/

For example, a decision which has the effect of conferring a financial benefit or detriment on a Committee member of $100 or more is said to be a decision having a

_____________________

4/ Section 87103:

(a) Any business entity in which the member has a direct or indirect investment worth more than $1,000;

(b) Any real property interest in which the member has a direct or indirect interest worth more than $1,000;

(c) Any source of income aggregating $250 or more, received by or promised to the member within 12 months prior to when the decision is made; or

(d) Any business entity in which the member is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee or holds any position of management.

5/ The issue of what is a significant segment of the public generally has been addressed by the Commission in the Owens Opinion and the Georgene Barnes letter, copies enclosed.

material financial effect on the official, normally requiring disqualification. (Section 87103(c) and 2 California Administrative Code Section 18702(b)(3)(A).) However, if the decision in question is whether or not to require all of the landowners in a District to construct a matching awning on the front of their building at a cost of $100 each, then all of the landowners are similarly affected and the owner-representative is not required to disqualify himself or herself from making, or participating in the decision.

If a decision will have an effect on a Committee member's interest that is distinguishable from its effect on the public generally or a significant segment of the public, and if it is reasonably foreseeable that the effect on the official's interest will be a material one, then the official is required to disqualify himself or herself from making, or participating in the making of, the decision.6/

For example, if the Review Committee is considering whether or not to allow an owner-representative to build on his or her own property, disqualification is required because such a decision will foreseeably affect the fair market value of the property to the extent described in 2 California Administrative Code Section 18702(b)(2).7/

Many of the decisions that the Review Committees will consider are not the type of decisions which will materially affect the members' financial interests. For instance, a decision on whether to forbid an owner-representative from painting his or her home green, and to require the owner, instead, to paint it white, is a decision having a negligible effect on the owner's financial interests. Thus, disqualification is not prescribed.

Finally, if the City of Pasadena choses to keep the owner-representative positions non-voting, this will not be sufficient to avoid conflicts of interests under the Act. If disqualification is required, an owner-representative may not make, or participate in the making of, a decision. 2 California Administrative Code Section 18700(c) defines the type of conduct which is included in this prohibition.

6/ For the specific amounts, see 2 California Administrative Code Section 18702(b)(2).

7/ See footnote 3.

Ms. Evelyn M. Finn  
August 6, 1982  

I hope that I have clarified the requirements of the Political Reform Act for you. In my hypotheticals, I have applied the disqualification provisions of the Act only in the context of the owner-representatives, but please remember that the other members of the Review Committees are also subject to these provisions. I am including a letter I wrote to the Pasadena City Attorney, Victor Kaleta, in November 1981, which I feel will be helpful to you in applying the disqualification provisions to the other Committee members.

embedding.qa_text: CONCLUSION: This is supplementary information to the Agenda Report dated 5/5/82.
embedding.first_500_words: August 6, 1982 Ms. Evelyn M. Finn Pasadena City Attorney 100 North Garfield Avenue Pasadena, CA 91109 Re: Our No. A-82-117 Dear Ms. Finn: Thank you for your letter requesting advice on behalf of the City of Pasadena. The facts, as I understand them, are that the City is considering amending its Zoning Plan and Code to allow the creation of Landmark Districts in areas of the City which are architecturally and/or historically significant. Each Landmark District must be composed of at least ten contiguous1/ properties which meet the requirements for listing in the National Register, or which are essential to the integrity of the area. Each Landmark District will have a Landmark Design Review Committee composed of one member each from the Cultural Heritage Commission, the Planning Commission and the Design Review Committee, as well as an architect, a realtor and an owner of property within the District (to be referred to as an "owner-representative"). The function of a Review Committee is to maintain harmony among the structures in an area, paying particular attention to their exterior design and such things as scale, height, spacing, roof shape, style, materials, colors and texture. To fulfill this goal, the Review Committee must approve any demolition, removal, 1/ Contiguous properties include those separated by streets, alleys and other public right of ways. construction, alteration, move or change in the appearance of any structure, portion of a structure, or other natural or man-made feature visible from the street. In order to avoid any conflicts of interest by the owner-representatives sitting on the Review Committees, the City proposes that these positions be non-voting. Your question is whether this is the correct approach under the Political Reform Act. Discussion The Political Reform Act does not require that the positions held by the owner-representatives be non-voting. Rather, the Act requires individual public officials to disqualify themselves from specific decisions in which they have a conflict of interest. The determination as to whether or not disqualification is required can only be made after considering the facts surrounding each decision. The conflict of interest provisions of the Act prohibit a public official from making, or participating in the making of, any governmental decision in which the official knows or has reason to know that he or she has a financial interest. (Government Code Section 871002/.) This prohibition applies to all of the members of the Design Review Committees, not just the owner-representatives (See Section 82048) and it prohibits them not only from voting on a decision, but also from participating, in any way, in the decision making process. The individual Review Committee members have a "financial interest" in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material effect,3/ distinguishable from its effect on the public 2/ Hereinafter all statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. 3/ See the enclosed copy of 2 California Administrative Code Section 18702 for the meaning of "material financial effect." Ms. Evelyn M. Finn August 6,
